cases.title: Uninsured, $80k for two appendix surgeries; charity care denied over one overtime month
cases.persona: Uninsured veteran, self-pay. Had an emergency appendectomy, then a second surgery for a complication. Hospital charity care and the state program both denied him because one month of overtime put his income ~$200 over the cutoff. Plain-spoken, stressed, doesn't know the system.

turns.text: I'm uninsured and I got hit with a huge hospital bill after my appendix burst. They did one surgery, then a few days later they had to go back in and do a second one because something went wrong with the first. Now I owe over $80,000. I tried to get their financial assistance and also a Colorado program but both said no — apparently I made about $200 too much last year, but that was only because I picked up a bunch of overtime one month. I don't have this kind of money. What do I even do?

doc.name: hospital-bill.pdf
lines: Financial assistance: DENIED (income over CICP/charity limit)

turns.text: So what do I actually do first? I'm a veteran if that matters.

cases.title: Uninsured: Good Faith Estimate $50,560, actual bill $97,998 (GFE to PPDR trigger)
cases.persona: Uninsured woman, self-pay. Got a written Good Faith Estimate before her procedure for about $50k, then the bill came in nearly double. Hospital says 'complications.' Doesn't know she has a federal dispute right. Confused, feels misled.

turns.text: Before my surgery the hospital gave me a written estimate that said it would be around $50,000 since I don't have insurance. The actual bill just came and it's almost $98,000 — basically double. When I called they just said there were 'complications' so it cost more. That doesn't seem right to me, the estimate was the whole reason I went ahead. Is there anything I can do or am I just stuck paying it?
OK so what's the actual first step — do I just call them again, or is there a real process?

cases.title: Hospital forgave 100%, but physician groups billed $5k+ (the 501(r) multi-biller loophole)
cases.persona: Uninsured student, self-pay. The hospital forgave her entire ~$100k facility bill under its charity policy, but separate bills from the ER doctors, anesthesia, and radiology keep arriving and total over $5,000. Confused why she's still getting bills after being told it was all forgiven.

turns.text: I went to the ER last year with no insurance. The hospital ended up forgiving the whole bill — like $100,000 — under their charity care program, which was a huge relief. But I keep getting these separate smaller bills from an ER doctors' group, an anesthesia group, and a radiology company, and together it's over $5,000. I thought everything was forgiven? Why am I still getting billed, and do I have to pay these?

doc.name: physician-bills.pdf
lines: SUMMARY OF REMAINING BILLS — one ER visit, 11/18/2025
Hospital facility bill (Lakeside Hospital): $98,400 — FORGIVEN 100% (charity care)
STILL OUTSTANDING (separate billers, NOT the hospital):

turns.text: So what do I do about these three bills? Is there any way to get them knocked down too?

cases.title: Uninsured, sued for $12k; debt snowballed to $33k over 12 years; never offered charity care
cases.persona: Uninsured, very low income (~$12k/yr), self-pay. A nonprofit hospital sued instead of screening her for charity care. The debt ballooned with interest and her wages have been garnished for years. Worn down, scared, doesn't know charity care was ever an option.

turns.text: Years ago I went to the ER with no insurance. The hospital never offered me any kind of financial help — they just sued me. The original bill was about $12,000 and now with all the interest and court stuff it's up around $33,000, and they've been garnishing my paycheck for years. I only make about $12,000 a year. I feel like I'm drowning. Was there ever supposed to be a charity option, and is there anything I can still do?

doc.name: collections-summary.pdf
lines: Charity care / financial assistance: never offered

turns.text: What's the single most important thing I should do right now?

cases.title: Cash-price paradox: online tool said ~$1,400, real self-pay ~$8.4k, chargemaster $17,979
cases.persona: Insured on a high-deductible plan but effectively paying cash. The hospital's online price tool quoted ~$1,400, but the negotiated price came to $8,424 and her share is $5,169. A competitor's cash price is about $2,100. Feels the price is arbitrary and wants to push back.

turns.text: I have a high-deductible plan so I'm basically paying out of pocket for this. Before my breast biopsy I checked the hospital's own online price estimator and it said about $1,400. The bill that came is way more — the negotiated amount is $8,424 and my share is $5,169. The chargemaster 'list price' on the statement is almost $18,000. Meanwhile another clinic near me quotes about $2,100 cash for the same thing. This feels totally made up. Can I push back on this?

doc.name: biopsy-statement.pdf
lines: Patient: Nora P.    Service: breast biopsy, 04/15/2026
Note: hospital online estimator had quoted ~$1,400 for this service

turns.text: What's the best way to actually get this down — what do I say and to who?
